slides.title: Leveraging DNS for Carbon Aware Server Selection
From Measurement to Emissions: Assessing the Carbon Footprint of Traffic Flows
NetFridgeS: Enabling Dynamic Frequency Scaling on Network Switches through Carbon-Aware Routing
Report from Passau workshop - part 2 (Dirk Kutscher)

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: you can download the pptx version and see the graphs: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/slides-126-sustain-leveraging-dns-for-carbon-aware-server-selection/

chat.author: Michael Welzl
chat.text: Good morning Renzo!  If you could try to produce a pdf without these problems and upload it, that would be great - I'll approve it right away during the session.

chat.author: Michael Welzl
chat.text: (or send it to me via email)

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: I am not the author of this, I am just an attendant to this meeting

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: but if you want I can try to do that

chat.author: Michael Welzl
chat.text: I can try that myself, never mind!  I thought you're a co-author  :-)

chat.author: Laura Soares
chat.text: some of the figures from this pptx file are not showing either :(

chat.author: Laura Soares
chat.text: but this might be libreoffice's fault

chat.author: Michael Welzl
chat.text: I just made a better pdf and uploaded it: https://datatracker.ietf.org/meeting/126/materials/slides-126-sustain-leveraging-dns-for-carbon-aware-server-selection-01

chat.author: David Oran
chat.text: to save tine i didn't ask this for Mallory's presentation, so...I wonder how we capture the differential effects on resource usage of what would happen if people chose other options other than using the internet. The obvious example is resource usage for travel, but i suspect there are quite a few others that could be captured.

chat.author: Chris Adams
chat.text:
greetings folks. I' v grateful for the shared notes link above.
I was involved in the draft mallory presented, so I can add a bit of extra info to questions

chat.author: Paloma de Oliveira
chat.text: (on the impact of the internet) Although I've only skimmed the draft, my understanding is that it's a potentially very necessary tool/framework to inform technological development – ​​as opposed to considering sustainability as research done after the damage has already been done. I also see it as an opening for dialogue and consensus towards an integrated technical-social-ecological system.

chat.author: Ali Rezaki
chat.text: Thanks very much Mallory for the presentation. Chris, you are indeed welcome  to add more perspectives. Since E-impact program times there have been several contributions to define a common terminology and problem space. May be these efforts could be merged and then taking it a step further, research questions and related state of art could be added leading to IRTF and SUSTAIN RG related research agenda topics perhaps. SUSTAIN RG charter is indeed interdisciplinary and inclusive. Many thanks again to both of you for your contributions.

chat.author: Ali Rezaki
chat.text: @Paloma: Thanks very much! We welcome socio-technical research perspectives. I encourage all to go on our mailing list and propose materials, research questions or proposals.

chat.author: David Oran
chat.text: At the risk of being too controversial, I suspect much of this is dominated by both "known unknowns" and "unknown unknowns". Here's a (poorly justified) example. Somebody spends tons of energy of carbon-intensive AI computations to model the battery performance of zinc-air batteries versus lithium-ion, which if adopted would shift environmentally damaging lithium mining to less damaging zinc mining. What's the win/lose balance here.
